summary: Joel Spolsky shares a satirical excerpt from a Meetup recruiting page that parodies Google's famously insular corporate culture. The brief post lets the absurd quote speak for itself, mocking how Big Tech companies create self-contained bubbles where even mundane services like haircuts are branded and elitist. The implication is that this kind of environment is more cult-like than appealing.
key_points: Big tech companies can create insular cultures where employees rarely need to interact with the outside world
The obsession with credentialism extends to absurd extremes when even haircutters are evaluated by IQ percentiles
Corporate perks can cross the line from attractive benefits into parody-worthy excess
money_quotes: At Google, you get your free Google-approved haircut at the Googleplex by a Googler with an IQ in the 99% percentile of all haircutters.
The average Googler Haircutter has over 45 RSS feeds in their Google Reader.
The walls are very clean, and you will not need to interact with non-Googlers.
key_insight: When a company's culture becomes so insular that even getting a haircut is branded and credentialed, it has crossed from workplace perk into self-parody.
